ground_truth.title: The Medical Minute: Getting help with mental health – a beginner's guide
ground_truth.author: Penn State Health News
ground_truth.main_content: TV shows and movies make it look so simple. Characters lie down on couches in cozy-looking rooms and reveal all their personal issues to doctors who just appear like magic. For some, the idea of talking with a professional counselor, psychologist or psychiatrist might seem like the very thing they need. For others, this process might seem scary or uncomfortable – or maybe even unnecessary. But however you feel, the journey from thinking about getting help to being in that cozy room with the couch ― if such rooms even exist ― can seem impossible. What kind of doctor do you need? What happens once you're there? And do you even need help? Those questions are well worth getting to the bottom of, according to statistics from the National Alliance on Mental Illness. One in five adults experience mental illness, and nearly 60% of them didn't receive mental health services in the previous year. Dr. Ramnarine Boodoo, a child psychiatrist at Penn State Health Children's Hospital, says taking the first steps needn't be complicated. Mental health might concern thoughts and emotions more than vital signs and measurements, but getting care isn't all that different from going to any other health care provider. Boodoo offers a guide to the uninitiated seeking mental health treatment, weighing in on where to go, how to get there and what happens at your first appointment. Do you think, feel or behave in a manner that affects your ability to function in a way you consider to be normal? You might need to see someone. Unless it's due to something in your life that's going to go away. For example, grief after experiencing a loss is perfectly normal. But when negative feelings linger, or if you can't pinpoint the cause of a troublesome feeling of behavior, a professional might be able to help you find a solution. Some common warning signs of mental illness include staying in a bad mood for a time period that seems too long, angry outbursts that outweigh the cause, and unusual thoughts or behaviors like feeling paranoid. Deciding to seek help isn't always easy and often involves stepping outside of yourself. It requires a level of insight. You have to honestly look at how your feelings and behavior affect you and people around you, and have enough maturity to say, 'There is something going on with me.' If you have a primary care provider, talk with them about what you're experiencing. Your family doctor should be screening for various mental health conditions including depression, anxiety and substance use disorders. If they have the time, they might do some basic counseling themselves. In any case, your doctor can make a referral to the right psychiatric help. Usually, whoever can see you soonest is the best choice. It doesn't matter which professional you start with, as they can all refer a patient to one another. Your initial visit isn't much different than what you might find at any doctor. You'll fill out forms, figure out insurance and discuss privacy. Then, once you see the provider, you'll discuss why you're there and talk about your history and why you're seeking help. Your therapist won't just be listening to the words you're saying. They'll also listen to the way you say them and how you react. There's always a possibility you and your therapist might not click, and you should never be afraid to make a change if it's not working. Many therapists have profiles available online, so you can do your homework ahead of time. Start with therapy. Unlike medications, which can have side effects, therapy is unlikely to be harmful in any way. Remember, there's no shame in needing help. The real tragedy is not asking for it.
with: Do you think, feel or behave in a manner that affects your ability to function in a way you consider to be normal?
Your family doctor should be screening for various mental health conditions including depression, anxiety and substance use disorders.
Usually, whoever can see you soonest is the best choice. It doesn't matter which professional you start with, as they can all refer a patient to one another.
Your therapist won't just be listening to the words you're saying. They'll also listen to the way you say them and how you react.
Remember, there's no shame in needing help. The real tragedy is not asking for it.
